sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE] in the kitchen and I can't do it, I'm attached.
[INAUDIBLE].
I'll do it.
Switch over the ringing tone on the answer phone.
I'll close the kitchen door.
You'll be in your room, will you?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Then he can answer the phone.
Now this one's off.
He's retired.
Yes.
Otherwise he might be bored.
Yes.
We're oldies.
Well, my dad's supposed to be retired, but he works all the time.
Don't ever let him.
He's an artist, and he paints day and night.
My mother goes berserk.
Yes.
No, that's good, [INAUDIBLE].
She runs around, has a wonderful time.
I run around.
You have to.
Because you see, often you--
My name is Odette Ross, spelled O-D-E-T-T-E.
Ross, R-O-S-S, as in sugar.
My name at birth, Rubinstein.
R-U-B-I-N-S-T-E-I-N.
Yes.
My first names are Odette Alice.
The 17th of May, 1927.
I was born in Antwerp, Belgium.
Yes.
As you know, I was born in 1927.
I had a brother who was two and a half years older than myself.
And we were the product of immigration from Poland at the end of the last century on my mother's side and the beginning of the first decade of this century on my father's side.
My mother was born in Belgium of Polish parents who, as I said, came to England towards the end of the last century.
My father was Polish-born and came to Belgium before the First World War.
My father was a diamond merchant.
He came to Belgium.

He told me not so much because of the anti-Semitism in Poland but because they were very poor, and they'd been told that the pavements of Antwerp were paved with diamonds, which was not actually so, so there was a lot of immigration to Belgium.
My maternal grandfather came earlier and became a very wealthy diamond merchant.
Unfortunately, he lost a lot of money during the First World War.
My father started-- my father had been schooled in Poland.
He left school at the age of 13, but he was really-- he should have been an academic, which is probably why my brother and my son became one.
But unfortunately, he left school at the age of 18.
And he still quoted Latin just before he died.
But he started working as a diamond cleaver, which is some sort of diamond cutter, and eventually became a diamond merchant.
And by the time I was born, my brother were born, we were-- they were comfortable, middle-class business people.
My four grandparents lived in Antwerp.
Yes.
Yes.
They're all actually in the family plan.
My father was one of six, and my mother, I think, was one of six.
No.
We were, we were secular Jews, but we were very Jewish-orientated.
I think by the fact that the diamond community was a very small and a very close community, one was very much that way orientated.
But we were secular Jews.
My father was very good towards other people and helped both his ugly younger sisters to get married.
But we weren't designed as family, not in the sort of [NON-ENGLISH] manner.
My father used to say that it's the duty of a Jew to give money to another Jew to send a third Jew to Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We both went to a private school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The main reason is that Antwerp was beginning to be Flemish-orientated, and my parents wanted us brought up in French.
It was a very liberal school.
And a lot of the teachers--
No.
The teachers and, and the headmasters had a very rough time during the war.
In fact, two of the teachers were shot by the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lead a very sheltered life, I would say.
My brother was taught just enough Hebrew to have a bar mitzvah, and I wasn't even taught Hebrew.
We kept just the High Holidays, but that was about all.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Until German Jews started coming out, I wasn't really aware of anti-Semitism.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- I had a very close girlfriend who, to my amazement, her father turned out to be a Nazi when the war broke out in 1940, and he came out in his [INAUDIBLE] uniform.
And also we-- as children we used to have-- we used to play dramatics and have a little dramatic society.
And we once asked-- I asked a girlfriend, you should come to tea to do a rehearsal.
And she told me that she was not allowed to go to a Jewish home.
I remember this, so it must have had some impact but not tremendously.
Yes, but also that was the day of the war.
I didn't know before that.
In fact, I have never seen her since.
But we escaped anyway.
We left Antwerp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became conscious of the political situation [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because we heard of Kristallnacht.
We didn't have a television, but we knew about it, although it was, it was remote.
It wasn't on our doorstep.
And German professors started-- particularly professors in academia-- started coming out of Germany and knocking on doors asking for jobs.
Which was, I remember, a very sad situation, because these people weren't trained to be in the diamond business or anything.
My father helped whenever he-- when he could.
Yes.
Yes.
The German Jewish people, of course.
And there weren't any organized-- charitable organizations at the time.
And presumably, as the German Jews did when they came here, they took job as au pairs or anything in order to survive.
Yes, we did.
But my father, whom I rather worshiped and who was an intelligent man, had blinkers as far as that was concerned.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was very aware of what was going on.
Being in the diamond business, he would have had the opportunity of leaving, of going to America and always spoke about tomorrow, next week, next month, but we never did.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the war broke out in September '39 between Great Britain and Germany, when Poland was invaded, it was then too late for my parents to try and get-- suddenly get to England.

I say that because people in the diamond trade found it easier to move than people who had factories or other things, which is why there are quite a number of Jews in the jewelry and in the diamond business, because you could take whatever little fortune you had in your hand and just get away.
And I think it is because we were the diamond business I'm here to tell-- alive and here to tell the story.
Well, that was-- yes.
That was afterwards.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From September 1939 to May 1940 when Germany invaded Benelux [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we still led a very comfortable life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and somehow thought, although we knew that Belgium was engaged in the First World War, that perhaps the Germans wouldn't get any further.
They wouldn't get to France.
And there was the imaginary line, the Siegfried Line.
Anyway, one fine morning on May the 10th, 1940, we heard guns.
And my father said, don't worry children, it's only a thunderstorm.
He then listened to the radio and realized that the Germans had invaded, or were invading Benelux.
And he grew quite pale and he was absolutely horrified.
I remember him rushing to the Diamond Club either to speak to other people or perhaps to get hold of his little fortune of whatever he had and came back and said we were going to move to the seaside.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The reasoning behind that was that in the First World War, parts of Belgium weren't occupied, and the Maginot Line had been built and the Siegfried Line, and somehow the Belgians kidded themselves that because of the imaginary lines they wouldn't be able-- the Germans wouldn't be able to get any further.

There was also an unwritten agreement within the diamond community that if there were trouble, they would all go to Cognac near Bordeaux, where the diamond [INAUDIBLE], diamond center, would evacuate and business would go on as, as usual.

This was a very important factor for me because later on, when I was separated from my parents, I hung on to this unbelievable hope that we would all get together in Cognac, and if we were lost or separated, this is where we would meet within a few days.
Of course, nothing of this ever materialized.
My father was the eldest of his family.

So he rented a large home in Lapan, and his parents, my mother's parents, his brother who, with whom he was associated in business, who was a partner in his business and my mother's youngest brother's wife and two children were with us.
The brother was in the army.
We all went to Lapan where, I suppose, we just waited for the events to happen.
That was about an hour and a half away.
It was between Ostend and the Belgian-- and the French border.
And the idea was to get as near to the French border as possible in case things would happen and we would want to get to France.
I suppose one felt that the French army certainly would be very strong and that if one ever did get to France, one would be safe.
We went by, by car.
We hired cars, and we went.
My father didn't have a car before the war.
We were in Lapan for very few days because we heard that the Panzer divisions were approaching very fast and, in fact, we were already surrounded by the Germans, but we weren't aware of this.
I was asked that before.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I didn't feel badly.
I didn't know whether I didn't feel badly because later on worse things happened or whether I took it as an adventure.
I honestly can't answer that.
But were all at the seaside, I suppose, with the hope of going back to our home, you know, when the war is over or when the fighting is over.
But within a very short while, you know, we realized that things weren't that easy.
And I think then we all became very, very frightened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we talked about what would be happening next.
But I don't think we shared our anguish and fears.
I think we all, children and others, kept this very much to ourselves.
And also I was a child of a generation where children were, you know, seen and not heard.
If my parents discussed these things at night, which I'm sure they did, we really didn't join in.
My brother, I'm sure, was more involved than I.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All I know is that one morning about a week after we got there, my father said it was time to try and get to Cognac.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We rented a car to cross the frontier.
And my father was going to ferry his family backwards and forwards.
In one car, his parents and my other grandparents went and crossed the border.

The car came back, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in one of them there was my uncle, that is to say, my father's brother and his wife, his two daughters-- and it was a very large [INAUDIBLE]-- my mother, my brother, and myself.
My father was a big man.
He was tall and quite robust.
And he really couldn't get in.
And my mother said, well, don't worry.
I'll go across the frontier with the children, and then I'll come back for you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got to the frontier that night, and the French were very unpleasant.
They said that the Belgians weren't really fighting very hard.
The irony of it is that Belgium capitulated within two weeks and France within three weeks.
But, of course, no one was [INAUDIBLE].
There was chaos and there was panic.
And they told us that we would be the last car that would go through that evening.
They would close the frontier at 7:00 but that the next morning they would reopen it.
My mother suggested that my brother and I should stay with my uncle and aunt, and she would go back and fetch my father.
It may sound a bit strange that a mother leaves her children.
And this is where I have to fill in a very, very important part of my parents' life.
My father was forced to fight against Belgium and France and England in 1914 because he was a Pole under the Austrian empire.
And although he relented for a long time, eventually, in 1917-- he didn't tell-- he never told me this story.
My mother told me this after he died.
He actually had to fight for the Germans.
He tried everything to avoid it, including sleeping on straw.
And he got asthma, which was with him all his life.
But, nevertheless, he eventually had to fight.
Fight-- he didn't fight, but he had to work in an office in Vienna.
Consequently, when he returned in Belgium, to Belgium after the war and applied for naturalization papers in the '20s, he was refused naturalization because he had been an enemy alien.
At that point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland-- having a Polish passport was not a, a very welcome thing.
So my father became a stateless person.
He had a stateless passport.
Although my brother and I were born in Belgium, we weren't really considered Belgian because our Father was Polish.
Therefore, we had stateless paper.
My mother was born in Belgium with Belgian paper.
When she arrived at the frontier, she didn't show our stateless paper.
She said that we were on my father's passport, and that she was Belgian and her children were Belgian.
So they let us all through, including her.
And having been able to get us through without any problems because the French were very unsympathetic to Poles, she said to us, stay.
We will be there in the morning.
I think this is relevant because a lot of people ask me, how could your mother have left you with your uncle and aunt?
And I know this is the only reason.
Anyway, my mother went back.
She stayed the night with my father at Lapan.
And the next morning, no taxi ever turned up.
She couldn't find him.
She didn't know his name.
And the worst thing is she didn't know the name of the village-- of the frontier village where they'd crossed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
They were absolutely panic-stricken and tried to thumb lift from anybody and said, take me across, take me across.
And my mother told me that she finally found someone who-- I think it was a Frenchman who said, OK, I'll take you across if you say you lost your children, but where did you cross?
And she said, I don't know.
We waited all day because, of course, one had no, no news or no directive that-- no idea that the taxi wouldn't come back.
We waited all day, and they didn't arrive.
And I remember being very concerned.
But as I said, I had the hope that we would eventually meet in Cognac.
After two nights of waiting at the frontier, we were told by the French, you, you, you've got to go, you know.
Go on.
So we went to the station hoping there would be trains or cars.
And by that time, a panic had started, and the French were capitulating and everything was happening.
We're waiting at the station one evening, hoping to get a train that would take us towards Paris and then from Paris to Bordeaux and Cognac when suddenly we saw a lots of little nuns.
Well, these nuns were German parachutists who had parachuted into, into France.
We finally got onto a train, and we traveled a few miles.
Of course, the lines had been bombed.
I don't know whether it was the work of the parachutists or not.
But anyway, that was the case.
My brother and myself were with my uncle and aunt and the two, my two girl cousins.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We tried to thumb lift, but it was very difficult.
Cars were being bombed.
There was no petrol.
There was a lot of incendiary bombs, you know, everywhere.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But somehow we felt that we had to go out.
So we dropped everything we had, and we started walking.
And the ludicrous thing is 50 years later, we kept on turning around to see whether the Panzer division were behind us.
I mean, no one in their right sense would do that, you know.
You can't-- they were called Panzer division because they were so terribly fast.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we walked with other refugees.
Jews, non-Jews, French, Belgians, et cetera.
No.
We were going towards, hopefully, Cognac, and we thought that they would also come the same way.
I don't think-- we weren't aware then that-- erroneously-- that the Germans wouldn't catch up with us.
We were in [INAUDIBLE] and we were surrounded by them everywhere.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had the, the bombs falling down, and we used to hide in ditches.
Very much like the Pied Piper, you, you get into a state where you, you just do what, mostly what other people do.
Except that we were in one ditch and for some peculiar reason my uncle said, let's get across the road.
And we all ran across.
And as we ran across, a bomb fell in the other ditch, and people got killed, which is a little incident my grandchildren always find quite amazing.
I suppose so do I.
We walked and walked, and eventually we found a big farm.
And I think there must have been about 100 refugees that took refuge there.
In the middle of the night, we heard soldiers marching.
So there was that sound, and then we listened, and we realized it was the goose step.
At that point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the religious Jews, the ones who wore the payots and the beards, all tore up their passports.
My uncle didn't, because he was born in Belgium, and he wanted to hang on to his.
But I, I very much remember people tearing their passports and burning them in the straw.
No.
No, no.
No.
But although he had a very Jewish name, Rubinstein.
At about, I suppose, 1 o'clock in the morning or so, a young German soldier came in, flashed his torch and said, are you all Aryan, in German.
And we said, yes, and he walked out, which is amazing.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I think about it objectively, I can understand it all.
They weren't trained.
They were victorious.
They didn't know what to do.
There was no organized anti-Jewish laws at that particular point, because they were only too pleased to be victorious.
And most of these young Germans had never seen a Jew anyway, because they were, they were very, very young.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, we went into the farm, which had been deserted, and we found half-buttered slices of toast and half-eaten breakfast, which we then all sort of devoured.
Quite a lot of them, we sort of shared whatever we could find.
And we found a little dog there licking the jam.
And we just grabbed it away from him.
I don't think I had the jam, but people were just eating it.
You know, there was no food.
We were in the middle of nowhere, and they were sort of--
That might have been about five or six days.
Yeah.

But once the Germans had overrun the place, there was no point in-- well, we knew by that time or they told us and we believed that they were in Paris, and they were about to occupy the rest of France, and of course, France did capitulate.
So we stayed for a couple of days in an empty house, which we just went into and slept.
And then we decided, at least back home we have a bed and a home.
It isn't any better in France when the Germans are there.
So we were repatriated on German trucks, because the Germans wanted order.
They didn't want refugees all over the place.
They wanted them back in their hometowns.
And so we thumb lift back on German transport of some sort.
And went back to--
And we went back to Antwerp.
Yes, but I didn't go back to my house, because my parents weren't there.
I went to stay with my uncle and aunt.
Yes.
Yes.
This, this-- I will speak to you in one moment, I just wanted to fill in something.
We saw loads of prisoners of war.
Oh, I forgot to say that geographically, it may be interesting to someone, we were caught by the Germans between Amiens and Arras on the way to Paris.
We must have crossed the frontier somewhere south of Calais or Bologna, but even I don't know exactly where it was.
But we were caught by the German near Arras.
And we saw loads of prisoners of war being marched along by the Germans.
And I'm always horrified when I see how well the British treated the German prisoners of war compared to what I saw happening there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went into a shop, and we bought some cigarettes.
And we didn't have that many cigarettes, so we stood by these prisoners of war who were limping along tired and, you know, with a defeated attitude.
And we said Shalom.
And whoever understood it, we gave cigarettes to.
We weren't discriminating, but we only had a few, and we knew the Germans weren't going to treat them very well.
In fact, they were beating them.
They really were not treating the prisoners at all well, not according to the Geneva conference anyway.
And my little grandson likes this little story.
